goal_11.config.title: Building Productive Cities for an Urbanizing World
goal_11.config.abstract: Cities are the engine of global economic growth and offer greater economic opportunities, diversity in jobs, and more extensive infrastructure than rural areas. Yet, the benefits of urbanization are not automatic. As urban populations and cities continue to expand, strategic investment in urban planning will be essential to ensure cities deliver opportunity for all.
goal_11.config.citation: Wadhwa, D and Laesser, C. 2026. “Building Productive Cities for an Urbanizing World.” In Atlas of Global Development 2026, edited by A F Pirlea, D Wadhwa, D Mahler, U Serajuddin, M Welch, A Thudt, and M Lambrechts. Washington DC: World Bank. License: Creative Commons Attribution CC BY 3.0 IGO.
goal_11.config.inputs: Harris Selod, Benjamin Stewart, Nishant Yonzan
goal_11.config.homeFactoidText: urban residents live in slums globally
goal_11.config.homeTagline: Cities are the engine of global economic growth, but strategic investment in urban planning is essential to ensure cities deliver opportunity for all.
goal_11.content.#1.text: In just over six decades, the world has become predominantly urban. In 1960, just 1 billion (out of 3 billion) people lived in urban areas. Today, that number has surged to 4.7 billion—about 58 percent of the world’s population.[reference: wup]
goal_11.content.#2.title: The world is urbanizing, but growth is concentrated in some regions
goal_11.content.#2.subtitle: Urban and rural population, by region (millions)
goal_11.content.#2.source: World Bank staff estimates based on the United Nations Population Division's World Urbanization Prospects, National definitions. Retrieved from World Development Indicators (SP.RUR.TOTL, SP.URB.TOTL).
goal_11.content.#2.visdescription: This chart shows how the global population has evolved from 1960 to 2025 with an area chart.
goal_11.content.#2.keyFactLabel: of the world’s population lives in urban areas today.
goal_11.content.#2.scenes.#0.text: Although urbanization is a global phenomenon, it has not unfolded evenly across regions. The stacked view of urban and rural populations shows how the world's total population has grown over time.
goal_11.content.#2.scenes.#1.text: Unstacking these two groups reveals how each has changed individually. Over the past 25 years, [emphasis: Europe] [emphasis: and Central Asia] have seen little overall population growth, with some growth in urban populations and a modest decline in rural populations.
goal_11.content.#2.scenes.#2.text: In [emphasis: North America, Latin America, and the Caribbean], rural populations have plateaued while cities have expanded steadily.
goal_11.content.#2.scenes.#3.text: In [emphasis: East Asia & Pacific], urbanization has been particularly dramatic, with urban populations growing rapidly as rural populations declined. The urban population overtook the rural population in 2008, a shift driven not only by migration or population growth, but also by the reclassification of rural areas and expansion of city boundaries, which absorbed once-rural areas into urban zones.
goal_11.content.#2.scenes.#4.text: Meanwhile, in [emphasis: Sub-Saharan Africa], [emphasis: the Middle East, North Africa, Afghanistan, Pakistan], and [emphasis: South Asia], both rural and urban populations have grown. In Sub-Saharan Africa, the Middle East, North Africa, Afghanistan and Pakistan, urban populations have expanded at a much faster pace than their rural counterparts.
goal_11.content.#3.text: And urbanization is far from over. By 2050, in all regions with the exception of South Asia most people will live in urban areas.[reference: wup] But the future of urban growth will look very different.
goal_11.content.#4.text: The next chapter of urbanization will be defined not by ever-larger megacities, but by the rapid spread of smaller cities across the globe.
goal_11.content.#5.text: There is no single definition of a city. These urban settlements are often defined by each country’s national statistical authorities, typically based on criteria such as population size, population density, administrative status, and share of non-agricultural activities.[reference: city-def] A simpler definition by World Urbanization Prospects uses only population density and total population as the key criteria to identify a city.[reference: def] Let’s take a look at how cities have evolved and will evolve by 2050.
goal_11.content.#6.title: The number of cities worldwide is projected to nearly double between 2000 and 2050
goal_11.content.#6.subtitle: Number of cities by population size
goal_11.content.#6.source: United Nations, Department of Economic and Social Affairs, Population Division (2025). World Urbanization Prospects.
goal_11.content.#6.visdescription: This is a waffle chart. Each dot represents a city in the world and the cities are colored by their size category: very small (50,000 to 250,000 residents), small (250,000 to 1 million residents), medium (1 million to 5 million residents), large (5 million to 10 million residents), very large (over 10 million residents) cities.
goal_11.content.#6.scenes.#0.text: In 2000, there were just over 9,000 cities in the world.
goal_11.content.#6.scenes.#1.text: By 2025, the world had over 12,000 cities. Small and very small cities (50,000–1 million residents) drove some of this growth, expanding from roughly 8,700 to 11,600. Meanwhile, megacities, those exceeding 10 million people, also grew significantly, from 20 in 2000 to 33 in 2025.
goal_11.content.#6.scenes.#2.text: But this growth in megacities is expected to slow down over the next 25 years, with projections suggesting that there will only be four more megacities by 2050, bringing the total to 37.
goal_11.content.#6.scenes.#3.text: Instead, urban growth will be driven by an increase in the number of small and very small cities, nearing 15,000 by 2050.
goal_11.content.#8.text: Far from being just a demographic shift, urbanization can be an economic opportunity. Across history, countries have become richer as they have urbanized.[reference: econgrowth] Why? Because cities create the conditions that make productivity and innovation thrive.[reference: blog-jobs]
goal_11.content.#9.title: Urbanization is associated with economic growth
goal_11.content.#9.source: World Bank staff estimates based on the United Nations Population Division's World Urbanization Prospects, National definitions. Country official statistics, National Statistical Organizations and/or Central Banks; National Accounts data files, Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD). Retrieved from World Development Indicators (SP.URB.TOTL, NY.GDP.PCAP.CD).
goal_11.content.#9.visdescription: This chart shows a scatter plot between urbanization rate and GDP per capita. Each dot is a country and a trend line shows that with higher urbanization rates, GDP per capita is also higher.
goal_11.content.#9.textParagraph: Countries and economies with higher urbanization rates also have higher gross domestic product (GDP) per capita. Hover on countries to see how their urbanization rate and GDP capita has changed from 2000 to 2024.
goal_11.content.#10.text: Urbanization enables productivity because it benefits both industries and people. As populations concentrate in cities, the forces that drive productivity—that is, scale, specialization, and innovation—tend to get stronger.[reference: blog-jobs]
goal_11.content.#11.text: When firms cluster together, they can function more efficiently. Being close to suppliers, customers, and competitors lowers costs. Large, dense markets make it easier to find specialized workers and match their functions with skills. Proximity to other firms also fosters knowledge spillovers: ideas spread faster, boosting innovation and growth. Urban hubs are key nodes in trade and transport networks, reducing shipping and coordination costs for goods and services. Better connectivity makes it easier for firms in cities to access global markets and value chains, accelerating growth.[reference: quigley2008] This economic benefit from the concentration of people and economic activities is called agglomeration effects.[reference: jedwab2017]
goal_11.content.#12.text: There are benefits for people, too, as cities offer opportunities for economic mobility, more diverse occupations, better pay, and better infrastructure. Let’s unpack how cities offer these benefits to their residents.
goal_11.content.#14.text: Jobs across rural and urban areas span a spectrum of skill levels. Low-skill roles such as cleaners, farm laborers, and construction workers typically require no specialized training or formal education. Medium-skill roles—including clerical workers, specialized agricultural workers, and tradespeople—demand a moderate level of education alongside practical, job-specific skills. High-skill roles such as professionals, technicians, and managers require advanced education and expertise.[reference: skill-ilo]
goal_11.content.#15.text: Let’s compare the specialization of each skill in urban and rural areas. In the graphic, the specialization indicator is calculated for each economy by occupation and location (urban/rural). For example, the specialization for low skill jobs in urban areas is calculated in three steps:  first, we calculate low skill jobs in urban areas as a share of low skill jobs across all locations; second, we calculate urban jobs across all jobs; and third, we take a ratio of the two: the share of low skill jobs across all locations by urban jobs across all jobs to calculate the specialization. This process is repeated for each skill level and location.
goal_11.content.#16.title: Urban areas offer jobs of a variety of skills
goal_11.content.#16.subtitle: Specialization of jobs by skill level across rural and urban areas, latest year available (2008 - 2025)
goal_11.content.#16.source: International Labour Organization. (2025). ILOSTAT Rural and Urban Labour Markets (RURBAN), EMP_TEMP_SEX_OCU_GEO_NB_A.
goal_11.content.#16.visdescription: This chart shows the specialization indicator defined in the body of the story. Each dot represents the value in one of three levels of skills (low, medium, high) and either in rural or urban areas of a country. So each country has 6 dots in this chart. The specialization values are displayed on the x-axis and grouped first by urban and rural and then by skill, so you can compare across all six categories. The further right the dot is from one, greater is the specialization rate. The further left the dot is from one, lower is the specialization rate.
goal_11.content.#16.scenes.#0.text: Each [emphasis: dot represents a country or an economy]. If a job is more specialized than employment overall, the indicator is above 1 for that area. When the value is closer to 1, the job is distributed much like other jobs. Values below 1 suggest that jobs are relatively less specialized. This is reported for urban and rural areas and for three skill levels in each country.
goal_11.content.#16.scenes.#1.text: Let’s take [emphasis: Indonesia] as an example, where the specialization value for low-skill jobs in rural areas is 1.07 and in urban areas is 0.95. This means low-skill jobs, compared to all jobs, are 7 percent more common in rural areas and 5 percent less common in urban areas.
goal_11.content.#16.scenes.#2.text: Similarly, medium-skill jobs are only 5 percent more common in rural areas and 4 percent less common in urban areas.
goal_11.content.#16.scenes.#3.text: High-skill jobs have the starkest contrast: they are 30 percent more common in urban areas and 40 percent less common in rural areas.
goal_11.content.#16.scenes.#4.text: More generally, low- and medium-skill jobs are roughly in proportion to overall employment in rural areas (with specialization rates around 1.08) and urban areas (with specialization rates around 0.94). By contrast, high-skill jobs are strongly represented in urban areas, where specialization rates are far higher (around 1.3) than in rural areas (around 0.6). This highlights the role of cities as hubs for high-skill work.
goal_11.content.#17.text: In many low- and middle-income economies, even low-wage jobs (which also tend to be informal) in cities can offer pathways to build skills, gain experience, and move into better opportunities over time.[reference: wb2021]
goal_11.content.#18.text: Prosperity depends on how well countries attract productive workers, match them with the right jobs, and help them continue building their skills. Cities make this easier because skilled workers learn faster when they are surrounded by peers who have similar or complementary skills, ideas, and experience. The result is higher productivity in urban areas, reinforcing cities as engines of economic growth.[reference: wdr2013]
goal_11.content.#20.text: This diversity in skill levels goes hand in hand with a wider range of occupations. By concentrating commerce, industry, transport, and services in one place, cities create more varied labor demand than rural areas, where work is largely centered on agriculture.[reference: quigley2008] Cities can support both everyday occupations and highly specialized roles that rural economies cannot sustain. Larger cities, in particular, do not just offer a greater number of similar jobs; they reorganize work into more specialized and interconnected roles.[reference: task2002]
goal_11.content.#21.title: Urban areas offer a variety of occupations
goal_11.content.#21.subtitle: Specialization of jobs by occupation across rural and urban areas, latest year available (2009 - 2025)
goal_11.content.#21.source: International Labour Organization. (2025). ILOSTAT Rural and Urban Labour Markets (RURBAN), EMP_TEMP_SEX_OCU_GEO_NB_A.
goal_11.content.#21.visdescription: This chart shows the specialization indicator defined in the body of the story. Each dot represents the value in one of 10 occupations (further classified into three economic activities: agriculture, industry, and services) and either in rural or urban areas of a country. So each country has 20 dots in this chart. The specialization values are displayed on the x-axis and grouped first by urban and rural and then by occupation, so you can compare across all 20 categories. The further right the dot is from one, greater is the specialization rate. The further left the dot is from one, lower is the specialization rate.
goal_11.content.#21.textParagraph: Apart from agriculture, forestry, and fishing, all other occupations are more specialized in urban areas. This highlights how cities offer a far wider and more diverse set of work opportunities for workers.[reference: task2002][footnote: If a particular skill-level job is more specialized in urban areas than employment overall, the indicator is above 1 for that country or economy. When the value is closer to 1, the jobs in this skill-level are distributed much like other jobs. Values below 1 suggest that jobs in this skill level are relatively less specialized in cities.]
goal_11.content.#22.text: For many workers, urban labor markets also mean mobility. In cities, people can easily move between employers and sectors, and sometimes even between informal self-employment and wage work. The density of opportunities in cities makes it easier to keep searching for a better match between skills, preferences, and pay.[reference: blog-jobs]
goal_11.content.#23.text: This diversity of occupations also acts as a form of insurance. When one industry slows, workers in cities can often shift into other sectors without having to move away.[reference: blog-li] [reference: urban-sustainability]
goal_11.content.#25.text: Jobs in cities tend to be higher skilled, formal, and more productive; and that usually means higher pay.[reference: urbanpay] Cities bring together larger and more diverse labor markets, with more firms competing for workers, especially in higher-skill sectors.[reference: thickermarket] This competition, along with better infrastructure and job matching, helps push wages up.[reference: compet-pay]
goal_11.content.#26.title: In most countries, hourly earnings are higher in urban than rural areas
goal_11.content.#26.subtitle: Share of hourly earnings of urban population above rural population[footnote: A score above 0 means urban pay is higher, below 0 means rural pay is higher.]
goal_11.content.#26.source: International Labour Organization. (2025). SDG Labour Market Indicators (ILOSDG), EAR_4HRL_SEX_GEO_CUR_NB_A.
goal_11.content.#26.visdescription: This is a beeswarm chart with each dot representing a country. The x-axis shows how much higher or lower are urban wages compared to rural wages.
goal_11.content.#26.textParagraph: In 66 of the 79 countries for which we have data (80 percent), average hourly wages are higher in urban areas than in rural areas. For example, in Angola and Peru, average hourly wages in urban areas are 40 percent higher than rural areas.[reference: paydata]
goal_11.content.#27.text: Of course, city life is also more expensive. Rents are higher, and congestion and crowding come with real costs. As such, higher urban wages often do little more than offset the added expenses of city living.[reference: offset-pay] But for many workers and firms, the benefits still outweigh the negatives. Higher lifetime earnings, better job options, and more opportunities to learn and grow make urban areas attractive.[reference: city-better] But these benefits are not automatic; they depend on investment and planning.
goal_11.content.#29.text: What does urbanization actually look like on the ground? Take Addis Ababa, the capital of Ethiopia. In 1975, fewer than 1 million people lived there. The city grew dramatically over the next 50 years such that by 2025, its population was over 6.7 million.[reference: wup]
goal_11.content.#30.text: Its physical footprint grew just as fast. This can be measured by total built-up area: the land surface covered by human-made structures and surfaces, including buildings, roads, and paved land. Unlike administrative boundaries, which may include undeveloped land, built-up area captures the true physical extent of a city.[reference: builtuparea]
goal_11.content.#31.title: Built-up area in Addis Ababa has more than tripled in the last five decades
goal_11.content.#31.subtitle: Built-up area (km²) and population of Addis Ababa, 1975–2025
goal_11.content.#31.source: Pesaresi et al. (2024). Advances on the Global Human Settlement Layer by joint assessment of Earth Observation and population survey data. International Journal of Digital Earth, 17(1).
goal_11.content.#31.textParagraph: In 1975, Addis Ababa had nearly 39 square kilometers of built-up area. By 2025, it had grown to 128 square kilometers.[reference: ghsl]
goal_11.content.#31.visdescription: This chart has two parts. One part shows the map of Addis Ababa and the built-up area over time with a time slider. The other part of the chart shows the growth in population in this city over the same time period with a line chart.
goal_11.content.#32.text: When such expansion takes place, cities have the potential to boost productivity and incomes. But this is not automatic. Cities that deliver on their promise are those that have planned and built infrastructure ahead of population growth.[reference: planning] Without that foresight, rapid urbanization can give rise to slums, overcrowding, and limited economic opportunity.
goal_11.content.#33.text: Slums: a sign of unmet demand
goal_11.content.#34.text: The growth of slums is a result of an unmet demand for land and affordable housing with convenient access to cities and their associated benefits.
goal_11.content.#35.text: Slums suffer from severely inadequate physical conditions, including overcrowding, unsafe housing, and a lack of clean water and sanitation that create serious health risks. Such conditions drive the spread of infectious diseases; worsen mental health through noise, pollution, and lack of green space; and contribute to domestic injuries, disproportionately affecting children and the elderly.[reference: who-slums]
goal_11.content.#36.title: People living in slum conditions
goal_11.content.#36.label: In 2024, globally
goal_11.content.#37.text: Today, nearly 1 in 4 urban residents around the world live in slum conditions, many of them in low-income countries.[reference: wdi-slum] With rising income levels, the share of people living in slums has declined over the past decades.[reference: wdr2009] However, in many places, progress has stalled and even reversed. For example, in Latin America and the Caribbean, the slum population fell steadily in the early 2000s, but has hovered around 18 percent since 2015. And in Sub-Saharan Africa, the slum population fell from more than 60 percent to just over 50 percent over the space of 20 years. But the share of people living in slums has been rising since 2020.
goal_11.content.#38.title: The decline in slum population has slowed in recent years
goal_11.content.#38.source: Urban Indicators Database, UN Human Settlements Programme (UN-Habitat). Retrieved from World Development Indicators (EN.POP.SLUM.UR.ZS).
goal_11.content.#38.visdescription: This is a line chart with years on the x-axis and the share of the population living in slums on y-axis. Each line is a world bank region.
goal_11.content.#38.y_title: Population living in slums (% of urban population), 2000–22
goal_11.content.#39.text: Even where the share of urban populations living in slums has stabilized or declined, the aggregate picture remains deeply concerning. In cities experiencing rapid population growth, a stable or falling slum share still translates into a rising absolute number of people living in inadequate conditions. Globally, the number of slum dwellers grew from approximately 840 million in 2018 to over 1 billion by 2022—an increase of more than 160 million people in just four years.
goal_11.content.#40.title: Globally, the number of people living in slums increased by 25% in just four years
goal_11.content.#40.subtitle: Total number of people living in slums
goal_11.content.#40.source: Urban Indicators Database, UN Human Settlements Programme (UN-Habitat). Retrieved from World Development Indicators (EN.POP.SLUM.UR.ZS).
goal_11.content.#40.visdescription: This is a waffle chart. It shows the number of people living in slums in 2018 and 2022 where each square of the waffles represents 1 million people.
goal_11.content.#40.square_represents: Every square represents 1 million people living in slums
goal_11.content.#41.text: Economic growth can help reduce slums by raising incomes and enabling greater investment in housing and infrastructure. But without careful urban planning and adequate housing supply, slums can persist, even in growing economies.[reference: slums]
goal_11.content.#43.text: As cities expand, so does the area of land that requires planning and servicing. Built-up areas are projected to grow by more than [emphasis: 50 percent over the next 25 years], largely in lower-middle income and upper-middle income countries, making the way cities expand increasingly important.
goal_11.content.#44.title: Built-up area will expand by more than 50 percent over the next 25 years
goal_11.content.#44.source: Pesaresi et al. 2024. "Advances on the Global Human Settlement Layer by Joint Assessment of Earth Observation and Population Survey Data." , International Journal of Digital Earth 17(1).
goal_11.content.#44.visdescription: This is a stacked area chart showing total built-up area over time - from 2000 to 2025 (actual) and from 2025 to 2050 (estimated). Each stack represents an income group.
goal_11.content.#44.keyFactLabel: increase in urban built-up area is expected in the next 25 years.
goal_11.content.#45.text: For cities to deliver on their productive potential, how they grow matters as much as how fast they grow. A fragmented city, where housing, jobs, and services are disconnected, infrastructure does not extend to where people live, and institutions lack the capacity to manage expansion, undermines the agglomeration benefits that make urban areas valuable in the first place.
goal_11.content.#46.text: Poorly managed growth leads to high living costs, sprawling informal settlements, and social exclusion, and holds back, rather than amplifies, productivity. But the answer is not to restrict urban growth, but to shape it.
goal_11.content.#47.text: This means planning ahead of, rather than reacting to, expansion, by extending road networks and services before populations arrive, encouraging efficient densities that make infrastructure investment viable, and ensuring land use connects people to opportunity.[reference: plan-city] Cities in Sub-Saharan Africa, where urban populations are growing fastest, face this challenge most acutely. At the same time, the pace of expansion in this region means that large parts of the urban environment have yet to be built, giving today’s planning and investment decisions lasting influence over future productivity and opportunity.
